name: Biography
description: Narratives based on real individuals, emphasizing formative events and character development while balancing fidelity and dramatic structure. (This graph has 18 nodes — one more than the 17-node standard — because it includes a second Anti-Pattern node to capture an additional genre-specific pitfall.)

nodes.label: Biography Promise
nodes.definition: The audience is promised intimate access to a real person's inner life â€” not a chronological record, but a dramatic excavation of the events, choices, and contradictions that made them who they were. The promise is revelation: we will understand this person in ways that historical record alone cannot provide.
entry_conditions: Story is positioned as biography or biographical drama
exit_conditions: Core constraints are established that fulfill this promise
typical_variants: Rise-and-fall biography tracing greatness and collapse
Portrait study illuminating character over event
Period-of-life biography focused on a decisive chapter
failure_modes: Delivering a Wikipedia summary without dramatic shape
Hagiography that flatters the subject rather than revealing them
So much historical fidelity that the human being disappears into event
signals_in_text: Selective focus on formative rather than comprehensive events
Private scenes that illuminate public persona
Narrative voice that interprets rather than merely recounts

nodes.label: Based on a Real Person â€” Accuracy Matters
nodes.definition: The subject is a documented historical individual whose life constrains the story. The narrative cannot invent key events, fabricate relationships, or fundamentally misrepresent the subject's actions and beliefs without violating the genre contract. Historical fidelity is not optional â€” it is the foundation of the audience's trust.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Subject's documented life is the structural skeleton of the narrative
typical_variants: Full historical fidelity with documented sources
Dramatized composite scenes true in spirit if not letter
Explicit acknowledgment of speculative elements
failure_modes: Inventing consequential events that did not happen
Attributing beliefs or actions to the subject that contradict the record
Using the real person's name to lend credibility to essentially fictional narrative
signals_in_text: Events correspond to documented history
Real relationships are rendered with fidelity to known character
Period detail is accurate to the subject's actual world

nodes.label: Formative Events Must Be Selected and Dramatized
nodes.definition: Biography is not a complete life but a curated argument about which events mattered most. The writer must select the formative moments â€” the events that shaped the subject's character, drove their decisions, and explain who they became â€” and render them with full dramatic weight, not as chronological checkboxes.
entry_conditions: Subject's life is established as the source material
exit_conditions: A clear principle of selection is visible â€” the narrative knows why each included event matters
typical_variants: Childhood wound that echoes throughout adult life
Pivotal professional failure that reorients ambition
Defining relationship that becomes the key to character
failure_modes: Including every significant event in the subject's life with equal weight
Events are included because they happened, not because they illuminate
The selection principle is invisible â€” the narrative seems arbitrary
signals_in_text: Earlier scenes are explicitly echoed in later ones
Some famous events are omitted because they do not serve the character argument
The narrative pauses at certain events to give them disproportionate time and attention

nodes.label: Character Arc Must Emerge from Real Events
nodes.definition: The subject's internal change, growth, or deterioration must be grounded in the actual documented events of their life. The dramatic arc cannot be imposed on the subject â€” it must be discovered within the record. The biographer's art is finding the arc that was always there.
entry_conditions: Formative events have been selected
exit_conditions: The subject's internal state visibly changes in response to actual events
The arc can be traced back to documented causes
typical_variants: Idealism corrupted by power and circumstance
Private wound slowly finding public expression
Greatness achieved then unmade by the same qualities that built it
failure_modes: Arc is imposed on events rather than emerging from them
Subject is static â€” the same person at end as at beginning despite everything that happened
Arc requires inventing motivations the record does not support
signals_in_text: The subject's language, behavior, and relationships visibly shift across the narrative
Changes can be attributed to specific scenes that preceded them
The arc illuminates why the historical record turned out as it did

nodes.label: Balance Between Fidelity to Facts and Dramatic Needs
nodes.definition: Biography operates in the permanent tension between what actually happened and what the dramatic form requires. The writer must navigate this honestly â€” compressing time, compositing characters, dramatizing undocumented private moments â€” while never crossing into distortion that falsifies the essential truth of the subject's life.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
Subject's documented history is the source
exit_conditions: Every dramatic liberty taken is in service of deeper truth rather than convenience or sensation
typical_variants: Compression: multiple real events collapsed into one dramatic scene
Invention within fact: imagining a conversation whose emotional content is documented
Omission: excluding true events that would distort the narrative's central argument
failure_modes: Dramatic needs override accuracy to the point of misrepresentation
Slavish fidelity produces a document rather than a drama
Liberties are taken for sensation rather than illumination
signals_in_text: Author's note acknowledges what is invented and what is documented
Invented scenes feel psychologically consistent with the documented record
The dramatic shape illuminates rather than contradicts historical understanding

nodes.label: Rise-and-Fall Biography
nodes.definition: Biography structured as a trajectory: the subject rises to power, prominence, or achievement through exceptional ability and circumstance, then falls through hubris, circumstance, or the very qualities that enabled the rise. The structure provides inherent dramatic shape because the peak makes the descent meaningful.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
Subject's life has a documentable rise and significant decline or fall
exit_conditions: Both the ascent and the collapse are dramatized
The connection between rise and fall is made legible through character
typical_variants: Political leader whose strength becomes tyranny
Artist whose ambition destroys their work and relationships
Business figure whose innovation calcifies into ruthlessness
failure_modes: Rise is dramatized but fall is rushed or treated as external misfortune
Fall does not connect to character â€” the subject is brought down by bad luck rather than who they are
Structure is so schematic that the subject becomes a morality tale rather than a person
signals_in_text: Early virtues are shown to carry the seeds of later vices
The peak moment is dramatized with full weight
The fall is shown as the logical consequence of the rise, not an accident

nodes.label: Portrait / Character Study
nodes.definition: Biography organized around understanding who the subject was rather than what happened to them. Event is subordinate to character revelation. The narrative circles around a central enigma â€” a contradiction, a mystery, a defining quality â€” and the dramatic engine is the progressive illumination of that quality.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
The subject has a compelling inner life whose complexity is the primary interest
exit_conditions: The central character question is illuminated, if not fully answered
The audience understands something about the subject they could not have grasped from the historical record alone
typical_variants: The public figure whose private self is the story
The overlooked figure rescued from misrepresentation
The figure whose contradictions are the subject
failure_modes: Portrait remains surface â€” we see what the subject did but not who they were
The enigma is explained rather than revealed
The subject is reduced to a thesis rather than a person
signals_in_text: Private scenes carry more narrative weight than public events
The same character quality is shown from multiple angles and contexts
The narrative resists simple judgment of the subject

nodes.label: Period-of-Life Biography
nodes.definition: Biography that focuses on a specific chapter of the subject's life â€” typically a decisive, crisis-laden, or transformative period â€” rather than attempting a cradle-to-grave account. The compression of time forces dramatic intensity and allows deep rather than broad treatment.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
A specific period of the subject's life is identifiably decisive or transformative
exit_conditions: The period is treated with depth
The relationship between this period and the subject's larger life is made clear
typical_variants: The crisis year that changed everything
The formative youth before public life began
The late period of reckoning and summation
failure_modes: The period is arbitrarily chosen with no argument for why it is decisive
The period is treated in isolation with no sense of before and after
Depth promised but the narrative still rushes through events chronologically
signals_in_text: Explicit or implicit framing of why this period matters
The period has a beginning, middle, and end that function dramatically
Events before and after the period are referenced to establish context

nodes.label: Authorized / Hagiographic vs. Critical Biography
nodes.definition: Biography exists on a spectrum from authorized accounts (shaped or approved by the subject or their estate, prone to idealization) to critical accounts (examining failure, contradiction, and complicity the subject might prefer hidden). Each mode carries distinct narrative obligations and failure modes.
entry_conditions: Core constraints apply
The narrative's relationship to the subject's own perspective is established
exit_conditions: The chosen stance toward the subject is consistent and serves a coherent argument
typical_variants: Authorized account that still finds honest complexity
Critical biography that maintains respect without hagiography
Revisionist biography that corrects prior idealization
failure_modes: Hagiography that becomes propaganda â€” the subject can do no wrong
Critical biography that becomes a prosecution rather than an inquiry
Stance is inconsistent â€” alternating between celebration and condemnation without argument
signals_in_text: Uncomfortable truths are included rather than elided
The narrative voice maintains a consistent evaluative posture toward the subject
The subject's own account of their life is presented but not automatically endorsed

nodes.label: Real Historical Context Must Be Rendered
nodes.definition: The world the subject inhabited â€” its political climate, social structures, cultural norms, and material conditions â€” must be rendered with sufficient specificity to explain why events unfolded as they did. Historical context is not backdrop; it is the medium through which character acts.
entry_conditions: Subgenre pattern selected
exit_conditions: The period's specific conditions are shown to shape the subject's options and choices
Anachronistic values are not imposed on the subject's historical situation
typical_variants: Political context that constrains or enables the subject's ambitions
Social norms that explain behavior otherwise incomprehensible in modern terms
Economic conditions that determine what was possible
failure_modes: Historical period is merely decorative â€” costumes and sets with no structural function
Modern values are projected onto the subject, making them either a hero ahead of their time or a villain by contemporary standards
Context is so foregrounded that the subject's individuality is lost in sociological generalization
signals_in_text: The subject's choices are intelligible given the period's actual constraints
Period-specific language, concepts, and frames of reference appear in the narrative
Historical events are shown to impinge directly on the subject's life

nodes.label: Real Relationships Anchor the Drama
nodes.definition: The subject's documented relationships â€” family, rivals, collaborators, enemies, lovers â€” are the primary dramatic material. These relationships must be rendered with fidelity to the known record while dramatizing the emotional texture that the record implies but does not always state explicitly.
entry_conditions: Subject's significant relationships have been identified
exit_conditions: Key relationships are active forces in the narrative
Relationships change or are tested by events
typical_variants: The formative parent whose shadow the subject never escapes
The rival who defines the subject's ambition
The intimate partner who sees the private self the public never sees
failure_modes: Real relationships are present but passive â€” they do not drive or alter the subject's choices
Relationships are invented without documentary basis in ways that distort the record
Only professional relationships are shown; intimate life is elided or sanitized
signals_in_text: Key figures in the subject's life appear as fully rendered characters, not instruments
The subject's behavior changes in the presence of different people
Documented conflicts, alliances, and affections are dramatized rather than summarized

nodes.label: Public vs. Private Self Divergence
nodes.definition: Biography's specific dramatic resource is access to the gap between the subject's public persona and their private self. This divergence â€” what they showed the world versus what they were in unobserved moments â€” is where the narrative's most revelatory work happens.
entry_conditions: Subject is a public figure with a documented public persona
exit_conditions: Both the public and private selves are rendered
The tension or continuity between them is made dramatically meaningful
typical_variants: The celebrated figure who is privately tormented
The private person whose public face is a constructed performance
The figure whose public and private selves are surprisingly continuous â€” itself a revelation
failure_modes: Only the public record is dramatized â€” biography becomes journalism
Private scenes are invented without grounding in documented psychology
The divergence is stated but not dramatized â€” we are told the subject was different in private but never shown it
signals_in_text: Private scenes explicitly contrast with or complicate public scenes
The subject modifies their behavior, language, or affect in different social contexts
Moments of unguarded authenticity occur in private that are suppressed in public settings

nodes.label: Defining Moment Scene
nodes.definition: Every biography must dramatize the event that made the subject who they are â€” the moment of formation, decision, or revelation from which the rest of their life follows. This scene is the biographical argument's anchor: it explains the subject. It may be a single event or the culmination of a formative period, but it must be given full dramatic treatment.
entry_conditions: Core constraints are established
The narrative has built sufficient context for the scene to land
exit_conditions: The defining moment is rendered with full dramatic weight
The causal connection between this moment and the subject's subsequent life is legible
typical_variants: The childhood wound that becomes adult drive
The pivotal professional success or failure that sets the life's trajectory
The moment of moral choice that the subject must live with forever
failure_modes: Defining moment is summarized rather than dramatized
The scene is present but not identified as significant â€” it passes at the same narrative speed as everything else
The connection between this moment and the subject's later life is never made explicit
signals_in_text: The scene receives disproportionate time, detail, and emotional attention
The narrative slows down for this scene relative to surrounding material
Later events in the story explicitly echo or reference this moment

nodes.label: Crucible / Pressure Scene
nodes.definition: A scene of maximum pressure in which the subject's essential character is tested and revealed â€” the moment when circumstances demand the most from the subject and we see, under that pressure, who they truly are. The crucible does not have to be the climax, but it must be the point of maximum character revelation.
entry_conditions: Stakes are fully established
The subject's core character has been sufficiently established for the test to be legible
exit_conditions: The subject's response to maximum pressure reveals character clearly
The scene advances the narrative's argument about who the subject is
typical_variants: Public crisis requiring immediate high-stakes decision
Private confrontation with the consequences of their choices
Moment of temptation or capitulation that shows the limits of their character
failure_modes: The pressure is external but the subject's response is not dramatized
The scene reveals only competence or incompetence, not character
The crucible is resolved too easily â€” the pressure is not sustained long enough to genuinely test
signals_in_text: Scene is structurally positioned at a point of maximum narrative tension
Subject's behavior in this scene differs from or confirms behavior in earlier scenes in revealing ways
Other characters observe and react to how the subject handles the pressure

nodes.label: Legacy / Summation Scene
nodes.definition: A scene, sequence, or structural moment near the end of the narrative that articulates what the subject's life amounted to â€” not in the terms of official history, but in the terms the biography has argued for. This is the moment the narrative makes its case: here is what this person meant, here is what endured, here is what was lost.
entry_conditions: The narrative arc has reached or approached its conclusion
The character argument has been sufficiently developed to support summation
exit_conditions: The subject's life is given a final dramatic frame that is interpretive, not merely factual
The audience is left with an understanding of the subject's significance that the historical record alone could not provide
typical_variants: Death or late-life scene that crystallizes what the life built or destroyed
Final public act that demonstrates the subject's enduring impact
Intimate private moment at the end that reframes the entire public narrative
failure_modes: Summation is purely factual â€” what happened next â€” without interpretive weight
Legacy is stated in voice-over or title cards rather than dramatized
The scene contradicts the character argument the narrative has built rather than fulfilling it
signals_in_text: The scene echoes or reverses the defining moment scene
Language and imagery from throughout the narrative return in altered form
The narrative voice, if present, speaks with earned authority rather than journalistic distance

nodes.label: Intimate Revelation and Historical Gravity
nodes.definition: Biography requires a dual tone: the intimacy of close character observation â€” the sense that we are genuinely close to this person, that we are seeing what others could not â€” combined with the gravity of historical consequence, the awareness that this life mattered beyond the merely personal. Neither tone alone is sufficient; the genre demands both simultaneously.
entry_conditions: Genre promise established
exit_conditions: Individual scenes carry emotional intimacy while the overall narrative maintains historical perspective
The subject is treated as both a private person and a figure of historical significance
typical_variants: Austere historical seriousness warmed by intimate private scenes
Close character observation that never loses sight of larger significance
Tone that shifts register between public and private settings
failure_modes: Pure intimacy without historical weight reduces biography to gossip or sentiment
Pure historical gravity without intimacy reduces biography to academic chronicle
Tone is inconsistent â€” either the intimacy or the gravity feels forced
signals_in_text: Private scenes are written with emotional closeness and sensory specificity
Public and historical scenes are framed to show their larger stakes
The narrative voice maintains awareness of both the personal and the historical simultaneously

nodes.label: Wikipedia Page â€” Chronological Events Without Dramatic Structure
nodes.definition: Biography fails when it becomes a chronological inventory of the subject's life events â€” birth, education, early career, achievements, later achievements, death â€” with no dramatic selection, no interpretive argument, and no revelation of inner life. The events are present but the person is absent. This is the definitive genre violation.
entry_conditions: Biographical subject is established
exit_conditions: A dramatic principle of selection is applied â€” not all events are given equal weight, and the selection serves a character argument
typical_variants: Cradle-to-grave narrative with no scenes of disproportionate weight
Summary narration substituting for dramatized scenes
Absence of inner life â€” only external events are depicted
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: Each life event receives roughly equal narrative space
Events are connected by 'and then' rather than 'because of'
The subject's inner experience is never accessed â€” only their public actions are recorded

nodes.label: Hagiographic Distortion â€” Subject Idealized Beyond Truth
nodes.definition: Biography fails when the narrative suppresses evidence of the subject's failures, contradictions, or moral complexity in order to produce an idealized portrait. This violates both historical fidelity and dramatic truth: a subject without flaws has no character arc, faces no genuine crucible, and teaches nothing about the human condition.
entry_conditions: Subject is a celebrated or admired figure
exit_conditions: The subject's failures, contradictions, and moral complexities are included and given sufficient weight
typical_variants: Authorized biography that suppresses unflattering material
Narrative that explains away failures as external misfortune
Portrait that presents the subject's flaws as secret virtues
failure_modes: This node IS the failure mode â€” its presence indicates a genre violation
signals_in_text: All adversity the subject faces is externally caused
The subject never makes a genuinely wrong or morally compromised choice
Contradictions in the historical record are elided rather than examined

edges.label: Promise is grounded in historical fidelity
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are real because they happened â€” the audience knows the events had actual consequences
edges.effects_on_character: Character must be grounded in documented evidence, not invention
common_alternatives: Biographical fiction may take greater liberties but must signal its fictionality
anti_patterns: Using a real person's name and life while inventing consequential events

edges.label: Promise requires purposeful selection of formative events
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Selection determines which events carry dramatic weight
edges.effects_on_character: Only the events that illuminate character need to be included
common_alternatives: Some biographical forms include more events but still apply a hierarchy of emphasis
anti_patterns: Treating all documented events as equally worthy of inclusion

edges.label: Promise requires character arc discoverable in the record
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Arc gives stakes their direction â€” we understand what the subject stands to gain or lose
edges.effects_on_character: Character must visibly change or be revealed across the narrative
common_alternatives: Portrait studies may argue for a static character whose consistency is itself the revelation
anti_patterns: Arc is imposed on events rather than discovered within them

edges.label: Promise requires honest navigation of fidelity and dramatic needs
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Audience trust depends on knowing the narrative is not fabricating
edges.effects_on_character: Dramatic liberties must serve character truth, not convenience
common_alternatives: Different biographical modes permit different degrees of dramatic liberty, but all require transparency
anti_patterns: Dramatic shape imposed at the cost of misrepresenting the subject

edges.label: Promise requires dual tone of intimacy and historical gravity
preconditions: Genre promise established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Tone signals both the personal and historical significance of the subject's life
edges.effects_on_character: Subject must be rendered as both an individual and a historical figure
common_alternatives: Some biographical works favor one tone, but the genre expectation encompasses both
anti_patterns: Pure academic distance or pure personal gossip without the other dimension

edges.label: Historical record supports rise-and-fall trajectory
preconditions: Subject's documented life has a significant ascent and decline
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes shift from whether the subject will succeed to whether they will survive their success
edges.effects_on_character: Character qualities shown in the rise must be visibly connected to the fall
common_alternatives: Portrait study
Period-of-life biography
anti_patterns: Rise-and-fall structure applied to a life that does not actually support it

edges.label: Selection principle organized around character revelation
preconditions: Events are selected primarily for their character-illuminating power rather than narrative drama
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are the understanding of the subject's inner life rather than the outcome of external events
edges.effects_on_character: Character complexity is the primary subject, not accomplishment or failure
common_alternatives: Rise-and-fall biography
Period-of-life biography
anti_patterns: Portrait study that remains surface â€” action without interiority

edges.label: Character arc concentrated in a decisive period
preconditions: The most significant transformation of character occurs within an identifiable period
edges.effects_on_stakes: Compression intensifies the arc â€” change happens under time pressure
edges.effects_on_character: Character at the beginning of the period is measurably different from character at the end
common_alternatives: Rise-and-fall biography spanning the full life
Portrait study
anti_patterns: Period selected arbitrarily without argument for why this chapter is decisive

edges.label: Fidelity tension resolved differently across authorized and critical modes
preconditions: The narrative's relationship to the subject's own account is established
edges.effects_on_stakes: The mode determines what kind of truth the narrative is pursuing
edges.effects_on_character: Authorized mode risks idealization; critical mode risks prosecution
common_alternatives: Some biographies are neither authorized nor polemically critical â€” they pursue complex truth independently
anti_patterns: Mode is inconsistent or undeclared, leaving the audience uncertain about the narrative's relationship to truth

edges.label: Rise and fall require historically grounded conditions
preconditions: Rise-and-fall subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Historical conditions explain what enabled the rise and what accelerated the fall
edges.effects_on_character: Character choices are intelligible within their historical moment
common_alternatives: Historical context may be foregrounded differently depending on whether the rise or fall is the narrative focus
anti_patterns: Rise and fall treated as purely personal without the historical forces that shaped them

edges.label: Portrait study built from the subject's real relationships
preconditions: Portrait subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Character is revealed through how the subject relates to real others
edges.effects_on_character: Relationships are the mirrors in which the subject's character becomes visible
common_alternatives: Some portrait studies are more solitary, focusing on the subject's inner monologue or creative work
anti_patterns: Portrait study that renders the subject in isolation without the real relationships that defined them

edges.label: Period-of-life biography exploits public-private divergence intensively
preconditions: Period-of-life subgenre selected
edges.effects_on_stakes: Compression allows deeper access to both public and private selves within the period
edges.effects_on_character: The gap between public persona and private self is often most acute during periods of crisis
common_alternatives: Some period-of-life biographies are primarily public â€” focused on a specific professional episode
anti_patterns: Period-of-life biography that remains in public view throughout, never accessing the private self

edges.label: Authorized vs. critical stance determines how relationships are rendered
preconditions: Authorized/critical mode established
edges.effects_on_stakes: Critical biography may reveal relationship dynamics the subject or authorized accounts suppressed
edges.effects_on_character: The mode's stance toward truth shapes which relationship truths are surfaced
common_alternatives: Authorized biography may still render relationships honestly if the subject's estate permits it
anti_patterns: All biographies sanitize relationships regardless of mode â€” hagiographic failure

edges.label: Historical context makes the defining moment intelligible
preconditions: Historical context is established
The defining moment is embedded in a specific historical situation
edges.effects_on_stakes: The moment's significance is understood against its historical backdrop
edges.effects_on_character: The subject's response to the moment is legible given their historical circumstances
common_alternatives: Some defining moments are primarily personal rather than historical, but even these are shaped by period
anti_patterns: Defining moment presented without the historical context that gives it meaning

edges.label: Real relationships provide the pressure of the crucible
preconditions: Key relationships are established and under pressure
edges.effects_on_stakes: Crucible pressure often comes from or is witnessed by the subject's closest real associates
edges.effects_on_character: Character is revealed under pressure in the presence of those who know the subject best
common_alternatives: Some crucible scenes are solitary â€” the subject alone with a decision
anti_patterns: Crucible scene that is purely abstract without the grounding of real relationships

edges.label: Legacy scene reconciles or crystallizes the public-private divergence
preconditions: Public and private selves have been established and contrasted
edges.effects_on_stakes: Legacy is assessed in terms of both the public achievement and the private cost
edges.effects_on_character: The summation integrates both dimensions of the subject
common_alternatives: Some legacy scenes focus primarily on public impact; the private cost is implied
anti_patterns: Legacy scene that addresses only public achievement without acknowledging the private life's complexity

edges.label: Defining moment establishes what the crucible will test
preconditions: Defining moment has occurred and established the subject's core character
edges.effects_on_stakes: Crucible tests whether the character formed in the defining moment holds under maximum pressure
edges.effects_on_character: The crucible reveals whether the subject's formation was strength or fragility
common_alternatives: In some biographies the crucible precedes the defining moment â€” the crisis provokes the formation
anti_patterns: Crucible that tests qualities never established by prior character work

edges.label: How the subject handles the crucible determines the legacy
preconditions: Crucible scene has revealed the subject's character under maximum pressure
edges.effects_on_stakes: The legacy summation assesses the consequences of the crucible's outcome
edges.effects_on_character: Legacy is earned or undermined by what the crucible revealed
common_alternatives: Legacy may be assessed in terms of public impact rather than the personal crucible
anti_patterns: Legacy summation that does not connect to how the subject handled their central test

edges.label: Failure to select produces the Wikipedia anti-pattern
preconditions: Events are included without a principle of dramatic selection
edges.effects_on_stakes: All events carry equal weight, so none carry real weight
edges.effects_on_character: Character is never revealed â€” only actions are recorded
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid

edges.label: Prioritizing dramatic appeal over truth produces hagiography
preconditions: Dramatic needs override fidelity to uncomfortable truths about the subject
edges.effects_on_stakes: Stakes are false because the subject's failures are not real failures in the narrative
edges.effects_on_character: Character has no genuine arc because only the upward trajectory is shown
anti_patterns: This edge IS the anti-pattern â€” it describes what to avoid
